The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{Main|Gasin faith|}}
In Korea landlord gods are called Gasin
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See Also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]
==References== {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Regional deities]] [[Category:Chthonic beings]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Planned developments]] [[Category:Taiwanese folk religion]] [[Category:Formosan mythology]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Chinese folk religion in Hong Kong]] [[Category:Deities in Taoism]]
{{Shinto shrine}} {{Religious Confucianism}}

{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref> [[Di (Chinese concept)]] is the first character.
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong|Tudigongs]] ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and [[Landlord deity|landlord gods]] world wide.
==In China== {{Expand-zh|地主神|section=yes}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) “The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People” ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) “The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back” ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a [[landlord deity]] or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes}} Worship of Dijizhu (地基主) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Dijizhu when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Dijizhu are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（Ⅳ. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>http://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/wip/SXAUh</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/oEmFb</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{Main|Gasin faith|}}
In Korea landlord gods are called Gasin
[[Teojusin]] is perhaps more explicitly linked to the concept
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See Also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]
==References== {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Regional deities]] [[Category:Chthonic beings]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Planned developments]] [[Category:Taiwanese folk religion]] [[Category:Formosan mythology]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Chinese folk religion in Hong Kong]] [[Category:Deities in Taoism]]

Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Dijizhu when moving into a new building.

Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>http://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/wip/SXAUh</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>

[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/oEmFb</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.

They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong|Tudigongs]] ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and [[Landlord deity|landlord gods]] world wide.
==In China== {{Expand-zh|地主神|section=yes}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a [[landlord deity]] or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes}} Worship of Dijizhu (地基主) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Dijizhu when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Dijizhu are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>http://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/wip/SXAUh</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/oEmFb</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]]
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See Also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]
==References== {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Regional deities]] [[Category:Chthonic beings]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Planned developments]] [[Category:Taiwanese folk religion]] [[Category:Formosan mythology]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Chinese folk religion in Hong Kong]] [[Category:Deities in Taoism]]

==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult}} Worship of Dijizhu (地基主) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Dijizhu when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Dijizhu are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/wip/SXAUh</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/oEmFb</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]]
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See Also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]
==References== {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Regional deities]] [[Category:Chthonic beings]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Planned developments]] [[Category:Taiwanese folk religion]] [[Category:Formosan mythology]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Chinese folk religion in Hong Kong]] [[Category:Deities in Taoism]]
{{Shinto shrine}} {{Religious Confucianism}}

{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref> [[Di (Chinese concept)]] is the first character.
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong|Tudigongs]] ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and [[Landlord deity|landlord gods]] world wide.
==In China== {{Expand-zh|地主神|section=yes|topic=cult}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a [[landlord deity]] or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult}} Worship of Dijizhu (地基主) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Dijizhu when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Dijizhu are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/wip/SXAUh</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/oEmFb</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]]
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.

Household altars to Dijizhu are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.ph/wip/SXAUh</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.ph/oEmFb</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See Also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]

{{Shinto shrine}} {{Religious Confucianism}}

{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref> [[Di (Chinese concept)]] is the first character.
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong|Tudigongs]] ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and [[Landlord deity|landlord gods]] world wide.
==In China== {{Expand-zh|地主神|section=yes|topic=cult}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a [[landlord deity]] or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult}} Worship of Dijizhu (地基主) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Dijizhu when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Dijizhu are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230407201919/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9641</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230407040316/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9956</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.

Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a [[landlord deity]] or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult}} Worship of '''Tē-ki-tsú''' ({{zh|t=地基祖|p=Dìjīzhǔ|w=Ti⁴-chi¹-chu³|poj=Tē-ki-tsú|first=c, poj}}) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Tē-ki-tsú when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Tē-ki-tsú are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230407201919/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9641</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230407040316/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9956</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See Also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]
==References== {{Reflist}} [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Regional deities]] [[Category:Chthonic beings]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Planned developments]] [[Category:Taiwanese folk religion]] [[Category:Formosan mythology]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Chinese folk religion in Hong Kong]] [[Category:Taoist deities]]
{{Shinto shrine}} {{Religious Confucianism}}

{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref> [[Di (Chinese concept)]] is the first character.
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong|Tudigongs]] ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and [[Landlord deity|landlord gods]] world wide.
==In China== {{Expand-zh|地主神|section=yes|topic=cult}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a [[landlord deity]] or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand-zh|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult}} Worship of '''Tē-ki-tsú''' ({{zh|t=地基主|p=Dìjīzhǔ|w=Ti⁴-chi¹-chu³|poj=Tē-ki-tsú|first=c, poj}}) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Tē-ki-tsú when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Tē-ki-tsú are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230407201919/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9641</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>

{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref> [[Di (Chinese concept)]] is the first character.
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]] and are best known for making [[Renting|rentoids]] seethe.
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there, where they often demand a sacrifice in the form of a mandatory 1000% tip.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong]]s ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and '''landlord gods''' world wide.
==In China== {{Expand Chinese|地主神|section=yes|topic=cult|date=January 2024}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a landlord deity or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand Chinese|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult|date=January 2024}} Worship of '''Tē-ki-tsú''' ({{zh|t=地基主|p=Dìjīzhǔ|w=Ti⁴-chi¹-chu³|poj=Tē-ki-tsú|first=c, poj}}) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Tē-ki-tsú when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Tē-ki-tsú are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230407201919/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9641</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230407040316/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9956</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]

revision.comment: Added 4 {{[[Template:Bare URL inline|Bare URL inline]]}} tag(s) using [[User:BrownHairedGirl/BareURLinline.js|a script]]. For other recently-tagged pages with [[WP:Bare URLs|bare URLs]], see [[:Category:Articles with bare URLs for citations from August 2024]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref>
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong]]s ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and '''landlord gods''' world wide.
==In China== {{Expand Chinese|地主神|section=yes|topic=cult|date=January 2024}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a landlord deity or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand Chinese|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult|date=January 2024}} Worship of '''Tē-ki-tsú''' ({{zh|t=地基主|p=Dìjīzhǔ|w=Ti⁴-chi¹-chu³|poj=Tē-ki-tsú|first=c, poj}}) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Tē-ki-tsú when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Tē-ki-tsú are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230407201919/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9641 {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230407040316/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9956 {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See also==
* [[Sheshen]] * [[City God (China)]] * [[Yin miao]]
==References== {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Religious Confucianism}}

[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230407040316/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9956 {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See also==
* [[City God (China)]] * [[Sheshen]] * [[Yin miao]]

[[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto terminology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Tutelary deities]] [[Category:Chinjusha]] [[Category:Regional deities]] [[Category:Chthonic beings]] [[Category:Japanese folk religion]] [[Category:Planned developments]] [[Category:Taiwanese folk religion]] [[Category:Formosan mythology]] [[Category:East Asian folk religion]] [[Category:Chinese folk religion in Hong Kong]] [[Category:Taoist deities]]

{{Short description|Asian folk deities}} '''Landlord deities''' (地主神) are a type of [[tutelary deity]] worshipped in the [[East Asian cultural sphere]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Dragon |first=Tin Yat |title=Landlord Deity in Taoism 土地神明 |url=https://www.tinyatdragon.com/blogs/spiritual/landlord-deity-in-taoism |access-date=2023-04-08 |website=Tin Yat Dragon |language=en}}</ref>
They are low level deities that are considered below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]].
When people move into a new location they will ask the landlord deity for permission to move there.
[[Houtu]] is the overlord of all the [[Tudigong]]s ("Lord of Local Land"), [[Soil and grain|Sheji]] ("the State"), [[Shanshen|Shan Shen]] ("God of Mountains"), [[City God (China)|City Gods]] ("God of Local City"), and '''landlord gods''' world wide.
==In China== {{Expand Chinese|地主神|section=yes|topic=cult|date=January 2024}} In China Dizhushen (地主神) are considered deities below [[Sheshen]] and [[City God (China)|City Gods]]
The Landlord God ({{zh|c=[[:zh:地主神|地主神]]|p=Dìzhǔ shén}}) is a deity worshipped in Chinese folk beliefs who is analogous but is not to be confused with [[Tudigong]].
The tablet for the Landlord God is typically inscribed with two rows:
On the left: (in Singapore and Malaysia) "The Landlord Wealth God of the Overseas [[Han Chinese#Names|Tang]] People" ({{lang|zh|唐番地主財神}}) or (in Hong Kong and Chinese diaspora elsewhere) "The Landlord Wealth God from Front to Back" ({{lang|zh|前後地主財神}})
On the right: The Dragon God of the Five Directions and Five Lands ({{lang|zh-hant|五方五土龍神}}; [[fengshui]]).
The names are accompanied by a side couplet of various wordings that praise the virtues of the Landlord God. It is believed that the Landlord God has powers to help gather wealth, and the position of the tablet must be placed properly according to the laws of fengshui.<ref name="sbnum">The Encyclopedia of Malaysia, vol. Religions & Beliefs, edited by Prof. Dr M. Kamal Hassan & Dr. Ghazali bin Basri. {{ISBN|981-3018-51-8}}</ref>
In Chinese [[Spirit houses]] are called 土地神屋 or [[Tudigong]] House, representing a link between the concept and the concept of an [[Tudimiao|Earth Temple]] dedicated to a landlord deity or a [[Tudigong]]
==In Taiwan== [[File:Landlord God R2.jpg|thumb|Taiwanese altar for a landlord god]]{{Expand Chinese|台灣地基主信仰|section=yes|topic=cult|date=January 2024}} Worship of '''Tē-ki-tsú''' ({{zh|t=地基主|p=Dìjīzhǔ|w=Ti⁴-chi¹-chu³|poj=Tē-ki-tsú|first=c, poj}}) is especially common in [[Taiwanese folk beliefs]]. Many institutions such as government agencies and companies will honor a Tē-ki-tsú when moving into a new building.
Household altars to Tē-ki-tsú are very common<ref>{{Cite web |last=ChinaConnectU |date=2012-01-23 |title=Religion, Folk (Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教){{!}}Mínjiān zōngjiào 民間宗教 (Religion, Folk) |url=https://chinaconnectu.com/2012/01/23/religion-folk-minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教minjian-zongjiao-民間宗教-religion-folk/ |access-date=2023-04-07 |website=ChinaConnectU |language=en-US}}</ref>
Such deities are ambiguous in their nature sometimes ghosts and sometimes deities. Sometimes considered the souls of former occupants<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last=弘子 |first=植野 |date=1992-03-31 |title=台湾漢民族の死霊と土地 : 謝土儀礼と地基主をめぐって（IV. 祖先祭祀の諸形態） |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/index.php?active_action=repository_view_main_item_detail&page_id=13&block_id=41&item_id=575&item_no=1 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 |language=ja |volume=41 |pages=377–411 |issn=0286-7400}}</ref> Sometimes rituals for such deities is seen as moving the building from the yin world to the yang world,<ref name=":1" /> see [[yin miao]] for more info on Taiwanese beliefs on the yin world
Such deities may be linked to [[Goryō]] or people who died without relatives
Alternatively the tradition may originate with [[Taiwanese indigenous peoples]] and their practice of indoor burial, or burying people inside buildings.
== In Japan == [[File:Ōkuninushi Bronze Statue.jpg|thumb|[[Ōkuninushi]] the original owner of Japan sometimes described as a Jinushigami]]{{Nihongo||地主神|'''Jinushigami'''}}, also known as {{Nihongo||地神|'''jigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||土地神|'''tochigami'''}}, {{Nihongo||地の神|'''chi no kami'''|also read as '''''ji no kami'''''}}, or {{Nihongo||地主様|'''jinushisama'''}}, are [[Shinto]] folk deities,<ref>Kamata, T. (2017). Myth and Deity in Japan: The Interplay of Kami and Buddhas: Vol. First edition. JPIC[出版文化産業振興財団].</ref> or [[kami]], of an area of land (the name literally means "land-master-kami"). Their history goes back to at least the 9th century and possibly earlier. Originally, jinushigami were associated with new areas of land opened up for settlement. New residents of the land created [[shrine]]s to the local resident kami either to gain its blessing/permission, or to bind it within the land to prevent its interference with, or [[curse|cursing of]], nearby humans. Jinushigami may be either ancestors of the original settlers of an area, or ancestors of a clan.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110518230601/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=213 | archive-date=2011-05-18 | title=Encyclopedia of Shinto - Home : Kami in Folk Religion : Jinushigami }}</ref> They are also known as Landlord deities<ref>https://brill.com/downloadpdf/journals/jrj/10/1/article-p102_6.xml {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref><ref>https://archive.today/20230407201919/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9641 {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> and sometimes described as [[genius loci]].<ref>https://academic.oup.com/hawaii-scholarship-online/book/17762/chapter-abstract/175508683?redirectedFrom=fulltext {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref>
[[Ōkuninushi]] is sometimes considered a Jinushigami of Japan as a whole.
[[Hokora]] are often created for Jinushigami,<ref name=":0">https://archive.today/20230407040316/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9956 {{Bare URL inline|date=August 2024}}</ref> natural objects like trees are also often seen to be [[yorishiro]] or [[shintai]] for them<ref name=":0" />
The goal is to convince the [[Chthonic|cthonic deities]] of the ground to allow occupation<ref name=":0" />
[[Shinra Myōjin]] is considered such a deity and to have originated in Korea<ref>{{cite book |last=Kim |first=Sujung |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PVrGDwAAQBAJ |title=Shinra Myōjin and Buddhist Networks of the East Asian "Mediterranean" |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |year=2019 |isbn=978-0-8248-8173-3 |pages=30 |doi=10.1515/9780824881733 |access-date=2023-02-20 |s2cid=243035601}}</ref>
==In Korea== {{main article|Teojusin}}'''Teojushin''' ([[Hangul]]: 터주신, [[Hanja]]: 터主神) is the patron of the ground on which the house is built in the [[Gashin cult]] of [[Korea]]. She is also known as '''Jishin''' (地神), or 'earth goddess'. She can be seen as analogous to a landlord deity, [[Tudigong]] or [[Houtu]].
==In Vietnam== [[File:Chùa Pháp Giới năm 2022 (Ban thờ Thổ Địa) (4).jpg|thumb|[[Ông Địa]] statue at Pháp Giới Temple]] '''Ông Địa''' ([[Hán-Nôm]]: 翁地), '''Thổ Địa''' (土地), '''Thổ Công''' (土公) or '''Thần Đất''' (神坦), is the god of the earth and patron of the land on which the houses is built. He is one of the most commonly worshiped deities in Vietnam.
==Pop culture== In the manga series ''[[Kamisama Kiss]]'' by [[Julietta Suzuki]], the heroine Nanami Momozono becomes the '''tochigami''' of a derelict shrine.
==See also==
* [[City God (China)]] * [[Sheshen]] * [[Yin miao]]
